paragraphs.body: Deepfake at Stake: An Ethical Justification of The Technology “…the essence of technology is by no means anything technological.” --Martin Heidegger, The Question Concerning Technology and Other Essays, p. 4

Deepfake, along with other homogeneous AI technologies, have been the center of technological and ethical controversies ever since their very birth. Recent years, with the significant and unneglectable increase in the number of crimes relevant to Deepfake, the ethical critique of the technology skyrocketed in amount as well (Matthews et al., 2024, p. 344). Within this set of research, the suspicious attitude towards this newly invented technology takes a predominant position; as Cejudo (2024) suggested, the proliferation of Deepfake works is very likely going to push social trust to an edge and leads to the “post-truth” society where nothing could be trusted by no one. Therefore, in the first section of this paper, the topic being discussed is the potential, under many circumstances, devastating influence that Deepfake brings. In this paper, these possible implications will break down into three aspects: negative epistemological effect, negative ethical effect on individuals. However, despite all the potential harmful consequences that may be imposed on our society, this paper is trying to provide a valid justification on Deepfake. As Heidegger put it in the quotation above, the problem of technology never manifested from within, but stems from human use of it. Henceforth, the second sector of the paper argues that Deepfake could be morally suspicious but possesses no intrinsic ethical wrongness (de Ruiter, 2021). The demonstration is composed by two steps: first, we are going to clarify or making the reason why Deepfake is broadly deemed as dangerous explicit; second, we will further try to argue that Deepfake is ethically neutral, and in fact contains treasurable potentials in different fields. 1 The Negative Ethical Practices of Deepfake Suppose you are a financial worker in a Hong Kong firm. One day, after a virtual meeting with an allegedly “chief financial officer”, you transferred millions of dollars to a target account. All procedures are seemingly legitimate, nothing within it worths any doubting. But it finally turns out that the whole virtual meeting is fake, albeit the realistic faces and voices (Chen & Magramo, 2024). A few transition sentences, leading to the discussion of epistemological defect of deepfake. Emphasize how epistemology is related to ethics, and thus try to convince reader that the research of epistemology is indispensable at least on this topic. 1.1 Epistemological Defects as Prequel of Ethical Concerns 1.1.1 Epistemological Defect of Trust Quoting from Matthews, T. et al (2024), introduce several basic ways of testify the truth: oral testimony, graphs, and videos. Pointing out the point: how deepfake could eliminate the trustability of videos as well as graphs. >>the elimination of truth and reliability social wise. Use the example above, emphasizing the ethical dimension. Relating this concern to ethical issues at the end of this section. 1.1.2 Epistemological Defect of Right Continue summarizing what Matthew says. This section main continues the topic demonstrated in the last section>>first define what is Epistemological right, and the relevant concept epistemological character. Quoting from other academic resources, acknowledge that deepfake, as the ‘fake’ in its name, could be adopted as a way of fraud, forge footages, etc.. While an important thing remains—the question of consent. Briefly mentioning what it is and how it may become a factor that prevents deepfake to be ethically negative and echoes this point in the second part of the paper.

Closely connected to what has been articulated above. The defect of epistemological character and trust immediately imply the indifference attitude toward the videos on our screens—people in whatever videos or pictures are no long being viewed as living human beings, but objects of enjoyment. Corporate the criticism asserted by de Ruiter, quoting Kantian ethical works, lead to the conviction of ethical danger of treating humans as merely instruments. 1.2.2 Body as Identities Focus on another dimension of concerns. The target of Deepfake is usually face/voice, together with other components of our body. Quoting from de Ruiter and Merleau-Ponty, articulate the fact that humans are embodied (we will explain this concept in a plain and easy-to-get way). The simulation and sometimes even theft of our face and voice may constitute serious moral issues toward social individuals.

annotations.comment: The rough draft looks good! Please extend the shorter sections to enrich your argument.

paragraphs.body: 2 Redemption of Pandora’s Box: A Possible Justification Briefly conclude what has been written in the last section, while bring up some opinions that may not be defeated by the ethical misfortunes. These include: the benign use of Deepfake of imitating voice and appearance of deceased people, the question of consent. 2.1 What is Behind The Scene Various phenomena are attributed to Deepfake technology—bad ones, and also beneficial ones. According to de Ruiter, this implies that the technology is ethically neutral. Follows by specific illustration of this point, which I haven’t decided how to do it yet. Corporate with Kerner & Risse (2021), try to explore and defense Deepfake. Certain points are going to appear in this portion: the distinction between morally negative and intrinsically negative, and several points follow the scheme. This part will become a predominant part of this paper and demands genuinely detailed articulation, which will be implemented gradually. 2.2 Further Extension This section will list several possible applications (positive ones) of Deepfake, as Cejudo put it in his paper, the artistic works. To answer questions and doubts on whether AI generated products could be counted as art or not, use the example of photograph. Meanwhile, adding some suggestions on future regulation of Deepfake.

3 Conclusion Reference List Heidegger, M. (1977). The question concerning technology, and other essays (1st ed.). Harper & Row. Matthews, T., Kidd, I. J., Fox, C., & Saunders, J. (2024). The Ethics and Epistemology of Deepfakes. In The routledge handbook of philosophy and media ethics (1st ed., Vol. 1, pp. 342–354). Routledge. https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003134749-33 Cejudo, R. (2024). Ethical Problems of the Use of Deepfakes in the Arts and Culture. In Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (Lara, F., Deckers, J. ed., pp. 129–148). Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-48135-2_7 de Ruiter, A. (2021). The Distinct Wrong of Deepfakes. Philosophy & Technology, 34(4), 1311– 1332. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13347-021-00459-2 Chen, H., & Magramo, K. (2024, February 4). Finance worker pays out $25 million after video call with Deepfake “chief financial officer.” CNN. https://www.cnn.com/2024/02/04/asia/deepfake-cfo-scam-hong-kong-intl-hnk/index.html Kerner, C. & Risse, M. (2021). Beyond Porn and Discreditation: Epistemic Promises and Perils of Deepfake Technology in Digital Lifeworlds. Moral Philosophy and Politics, 8(1), 81-108. https://doi.org/10.1515/mopp-2020-0024
